title: Pelvic Organ Prolapse
chief_complaint: I feel a bulge coming out of my vagina. It's worse when I stand or lift things. I have to push it back in to empty my bladder completely. I've had 4 vaginal deliveries including a 10-pound baby.

physical_exam.Pelvic: Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification (POP-Q): Stage III anterior vaginal wall prolapse (cystocele - bladder descent 4cm beyond hymen). Stage II uterine prolapse (cervix descends to hymen with Valsalva). Stage I posterior prolapse (rectocele - within 1cm of hymen). Perineal body thin.
physical_exam.Urinary: Post-void residual 150mL (elevated - incomplete emptying from urethral kinking). Urinalysis normal. Stress incontinence demonstrable with cough (urine leakage).
physical_exam.Bowel: No fecal incontinence. Normal sphincter tone.

hidden_data.urodynamics: Urodynamic testing: Stress incontinence (leakage with Valsalva without detrusor contraction). Voiding dysfunction (elevated PVR). No detrusor overactivity.
hidden_data.renal_us: Normal kidneys. No hydronephrosis (severe prolapse can cause ureteral obstruction).
hidden_data.pessary_trial: Pessary fitted (ring with support): Prolapse reduced, patient voids completely (PVR 20mL). Stress incontinence resolved (pessary supports urethra). Patient declines surgery for now.

correct_diagnosis: Advanced Pelvic Organ Prolapse (Stage III Cystocele, Stage II Uterine Prolapse) with Voiding Dysfunction and Stress Urinary Incontinence
key_learning_points: Pelvic organ prolapse = descent of pelvic organs through vaginal canal. Types: Cystocele (bladder - anterior wall), Rectocele (rectum - posterior wall), Enterocele (small bowel - apex), Uterine prolapse (uterus), Vaginal vault prolapse (post-hysterectomy). Staged by POP-Q: 0 (no prolapse) to IV (complete eversion)

Risk factors: Vaginal delivery (strongest - especially forceps, macrosomia, prolonged second stage), Multiparity, Age, Obesity, Chronic increased intra-abdominal pressure (constipation, heavy lifting, COPD), Connective tissue disorders, Hysterectomy (for vault prolapse)

Symptoms: Vaginal bulge/pressure (most common - 'something falling out'), Pelvic pain/pressure (worse with standing/prolonged activity), Voiding dysfunction (incomplete emptying, need to reduce prolapse to void), Urinary incontinence, Bowel dysfunction (splinting to defecate), Sexual dysfunction

Diagnosis: POP-Q examination (standardized measurement system). Assess all 3 compartments with Valsalva. Assess for stress incontinence with reduced prolapse (occult stress incontinence - prolapse kinks urethra, masking incontinence. Repaired prolapse may unmask incontinence). Urodynamics if complex

Treatment: Depends on symptoms, severity, and patient goals. 1) OBSERVATION: If asymptomatic. 2) PESSARY: Ring, Gellhorn, cube, donut (support prolapse). Removable, non-surgical. Good option for nonsurgical candidates. Requires maintenance (remove, clean, reinsert q3-6 months). 3) PELVIC FLOOR PHYSICAL THERAPY: Kegel exercises (strengthen levator ani). Biofeedback. 4) SURGERY: Anterior colporrhaphy (cystocele repair). Posterior colporrhaphy (rectocele repair). Uterosacral ligament suspension or Sacrospinous fixation (apical suspension). Sacrocolpopexy (mesh - gold standard for apical prolapse). Colpocleisis (obliterative - for elderly, no future sexual function). Hysterectomy if uterine prolapse. Anti-incontinence procedure if stress incontinence (mid-urethral sling). Surgical complications: Mesh erosion, Recurrence (30% at 10 years), De novo incontinence, Dyspareunia
